role_hints.oews_basis: 27-3091 Interpreters and Translators 2.1% of wage bill in the record's 4-digit 5419 mix — but that mix is dominated by veterinary occupations from sibling 541940 (veterinarians 17.1%, vet techs 9.2%) and is unusable as a proxy; within an actual LSP the linguist share of the W-2 wage bill is far higher, and most production linguists are 1099 freelancers who never appear in OEWS wages at all
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~20-30% of W-2 wages (hypothesis; freelance linguists sit in COGS)
role_hints.note: Written translation is the most AI-displaced task in the economy — MT post-editing (MTPE) is already the default workflow at documented 30-50% per-word discounts; what persists is certified/sworn translation, high-liability legal/medical work, and transcreation. On-site and live interpretation is physical/real-time and holds longer, though AI speech translation is closing in on remote modalities.

role_hints.oews_basis: not in the contaminated top-10 — PMs are the operational core of every language services company; hypothesis est. ~25-35% of W-2 wages
role_hints.note: File prep, TM/termbase management, vendor assignment, QA passes and delivery are being automated end-to-end by TMS platforms with AI orchestration; client-side program management and escalation persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 2.3% in the contaminated mix; real interpreting firms carry large scheduling/dispatch teams — hypothesis est. ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Appointment scheduling, interpreter matching and telephony routing automate near-fully; platforms already do.

role_hints.oews_basis: 41-3091 Sales Reps of Services 3.3% in the contaminated mix; hypothesis est. ~10-15%
role_hints.note: RFP responses and quoting automate; healthcare-system, court and enterprise localization relationships persist and are the firm's real asset.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 6.6% (one of the few plausible signals in the 5419 proxy)
role_hints.note: Capacity planning and margin management automate substantially; compliance ownership (HIPAA, court certification) persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: long tail of admin occupations, est. remainder ~10-15%
role_hints.note: Invoicing (per-word/per-minute reconciliation), freelancer payables and compliance paperwork automate near-fully.

source_hints.source: Slator (Language Industry Intelligence) — market reports and daily coverage
source_hints.why: The industry's trade press; tracks MT/LLM adoption, MTPE pricing shifts and every notable LSP transaction — the single best source for this code.
Annual LSP rankings and market sizing with explicit AI-impact analysis; segments interpreting vs. translation revenue.

source_hints.source: CSA Research (Common Sense Advisory) industry surveys
source_hints.why: Long-running enterprise-buyer and LSP survey data — evidence on how much client spend has already shifted to MT-first workflows.

source_hints.source: ALC (Association of Language Companies) annual industry survey
source_hints.why: US LSP owner demographics, firm financial benchmarks and succession-relevant data for the small-agency segment.

source_hints.source: Slator M&A and funding coverage (and Slator LSPI index)
source_hints.why: Documents the active consolidators (Propio, Sorenson, Big Language Solutions, TransPerfect tuck-ins, LanguageLine/Teleperformance) and frequently reports EBITDA multiples on language-industry deals.

source_hints.source: Section 1557 ACA / HHS language-access regulations and state court interpreter certification requirements
source_hints.why: The statutory mandates (qualified interpreters in healthcare, certified interpreters in courts) that create the payer-mandated demand floor for interpreting — primary documents for the terminal-value mechanism.

capture_questions: Translation is priced per word and interpreting per minute/hour — MTPE discount schedules (30-50% off per-word rates) already show AI savings being passed to clients as lower prices, not kept as margin; what evidence shows any LSP holding price while its production cost collapses, and does interpreting's per-minute pricing follow the same deflation path as AI speech translation matures?

Who owns the client relationship — the LSP (contracted language-access programs with hospital systems, courts, insurers) or the freelance linguist the end user actually sees? Which contracts (statewide court contracts, health-system language-access agreements) are institutional and transferable in an acquisition?

What stops enterprise clients from bypassing LSPs with LLM translation plus an in-house reviewer — and for which revenue lines (certified translations for USCIS/courts, sworn documents, regulated medical device labeling under ISO 17100-type requirements) does a certification or liability gate genuinely block the bypass?

In mandated interpreting (Section 1557 healthcare, court access), the payer often reimburses per encounter at set rates — when AI cuts the LSP's delivery cost, do public payers and hospital procurement re-rate the contracts downward at renewal, clawing back the savings?
terminal_value_question: This is the canonical melter test: LLMs produce the product itself — translated text — at near-zero marginal cost, so the question is precisely which revenue survives as paid demand: statutorily mandated live interpretation (Section 1557 healthcare encounters, court certification requirements, ADA/deaf services), certification-gated document translation (USCIS, sworn/legal), and liability-bearing regulated content — versus ordinary business translation, which is already collapsing into MT-with-review at falling per-word rates. A researcher who scores this code on current LSP revenue growth (interpreting demand is still rising on demographics and enforcement) without separating the mandate-protected floor from the substitutable bulk will get terminal value wrong in either direction. What fraction of this operator's revenue sits behind a statute or certification, and what happens to the rest at mature AI?
special_notes: Role-mix contamination is total: the 4-digit 5419 proxy is veterinary-dominated (541940 is the giant sibling); 27-3091 Interpreters and Translators shows only 2.1%. Rebuild the V breakdown from LSP structure (linguists/PMs/scheduling), and note that most production linguists are 1099 freelancers — their cost sits in COGS, not the wage bill, so the 0.4178 labor share understates the true language-labor content AI attacks. This materially changes V: AI savings land partly in gross margin (freelance spend), not just opex.

Pricing-model deflation is documented, not hypothetical: per-word pricing with MTPE discount tiers is the industry standard, and per-word rates have been falling for a decade. Model pi_dist with the per-word/per-minute rate-card channel explicit; the operator keeps savings only where contracts are fixed-fee or mandate-protected.

Segment split is everything: interpreting (esp. healthcare OPI/VRI and court) is growing, mandate-protected and physically/real-time delivered; written translation is the melting half. Blended firm-level financials mask this — insist the run separates the two before scoring V, A or terminal value.

Consolidation is active and well covered (Propio, Sorenson Communications, Big Language Solutions, TransPerfect, LanguageLine/Teleperformance, court-contract roll-ups) — usable for B and M only, never as evidence for V or A (rule 2). Slator reports deal multiples; use those rather than generic services comps.

EBITDA normalization: LSP gross margins run 40-50% over freelance COGS; small-agency 'profit' includes owner comp (SDE). Also watch revenue recognition on interpreting minutes vs. translation projects when comparing margins across firms.

Boundary confusion: sign-language interpreting providers (Sorenson) straddle this code and telecom relay services funded by the FCC TRS fund; localization technology platforms (TMS vendors) are software 511210/513210; captioning/subtitling straddles 541930 and 512191 — Nimdzi/Slator market sizes include tech revenue; strip it before mapping to the code's $6.1B receipts.

Structure note: 2,193 of 2,674 firms are <5 employees and n_band is only 28 (the 100-499 class) — the roll-up universe is thin, and the biggest firms (15 firms, 500+, avg $211M revenue) are already consolidator-owned. Golden-set melter anchor: be conservative about cost-savings framing; substitution dominates.
